headline: Chips routed as money hides in the boring stuff
body_html:
Markets turned defensive on Tuesday as a sharp pullback in semiconductors dragged the broad tape lower, even while the classic safe-harbor corners of the market held their ground. Across the 200-name Shariah-compliant universe, decliners outnumbered advancers by roughly three to two — and almost all of the damage was in one place.

Chips took it on the chin. The semiconductor cohort fell about 8.5% on average, led lower by a brutal double-digit slide in Micron as the memory trade unwound and investors questioned how much of this year's AI-hardware enthusiasm was already in the price. It was the kind of session that punishes crowding.

The rotation had a familiar shape. Consumer Staples — the place money goes when conviction fades — topped the board, up roughly 1.8%, with Healthcare close behind. CDW bucked the gloom to lead all names higher. For a strategy built on quality and capital preservation, days like this are the entire point: defensive positioning cushions the blow while the high-beta names do the bleeding.

One note on the scoreboard: QARP's rankings will re-rate on today's price action, because a cheaper price means more upside-to-fair-value. But the underlying verdicts won't move until the fundamentals do — and a one-day selloff isn't a fundamental.

briefs.headline: Memory names lead the chip selloff
briefs.body: Micron paced a double-digit retreat across semiconductors as the memory trade unwound, dragging the sector down roughly 8.5% on average. After a year in which chips carried much of the market's gains, investors trimmed the most crowded corner of the tape — a reminder that 'priced for perfection' cuts both ways.

briefs.headline: Defensives catch the bid
briefs.body: Consumer Staples and Healthcare finished higher as money rotated out of high-beta growth and into balance sheets and dividends. It was a session that rewarded the boring — exactly the kind of ballast a capital-preservation book is built to provide on a red day.

briefs.headline: Energy stays the macro wildcard
briefs.body: Crude remained in focus amid Middle East supply concerns, with refiners reportedly reworking sourcing. Oil is the swing factor for the inflation path — and, by extension, for how soon the Fed feels free to ease — so energy headlines carry weight well beyond the energy sector.

briefs.headline: Cheap isn't the same as a catalyst
briefs.body: On a down day, beaten-down quality names screen 'cheaper' and their QARP ranks tick up. But a lower price is not a reason to re-rate. The Catalyst tag is built for exactly this distinction — separating discounts where insiders are stepping in from value traps where they're heading for the exits.
